good: The key to the cabinets is on the table.
bad: The key to the cabinets are on the table.
note: Singular subject 'key' with plural distractor 'cabinets' (agreement attraction).

good: The author that the critics praised was famous.
bad: The author that the critics praised were famous.
note: Singular head 'author' across an intervening plural 'critics'.

good: The path to the mountains was very steep.
bad: The path to the mountains were very steep.

good: The painting on the walls is beautiful.
bad: The painting on the walls are beautiful.

good: The list of the senators was long.
bad: The list of the senators were long.

note: NPI 'any' licensed by 'nobody', unlicensed under positive 'somebody'.

good: She has not eaten anything today.
No student has ever finished early.
bad: Some student has ever finished early.

good: The teacher did not assign any homework.
I doubt that he cares at all.
bad: I think that he cares at all.
note: NPI 'at all' licensed by 'doubt', unlicensed under 'think'.

good: Which book did the student read yesterday?
bad: Which book did the student read the magazine yesterday?
note: Filler 'which book' needs an empty object position; the bad item fills the gap.

good: What do you believe John saw?
bad: What do you believe John saw the movie?
note: Filled gap: the object position is already occupied.

good: Who did you say Mary met?
bad: Who did you say Mary met Sue?
note: Filled gap in the embedded clause.

good: Which car did she drive to work?
bad: Which car did she drive the truck to work?
note: Filled gap: object position already occupied.

good: What did the chef cook for dinner?
bad: What did the chef cook the pasta for dinner?
note: Filled gap: object position already occupied.
